data.title: Dollar Tree: Attractive Buy With Signs Of A Rebound
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Dollar Tree, Inc (NYSE: NASDAQ:DLTR) is one of North America's most prominent retail stores, in operation for over three decades. With 15500+ store locations, over 200,000 employees, and more than 193,000 associates, Dollar Tree provides value to customers with its product portfolio, including household supplies, food and snacks, school supplies, and dining materials at low price-points. With the company at a slight discount, Dollar Tree is a pivotal long-term investment with growth potential.

Acknowledging that the company underperformed in the past few years, the Dollar Tree management is keen on rectifying this with the Family Dollar divestiture, with the deal expected to take place in June for $1.007 billion. The deal will ease burden off Dollar Tree’s operating margins, while also enhancing the overall profitability and growth of its flagship stores. The Multi-Price strategy is also a critical growth driver to grow Dollar Tree’s customer base, profitability, and overall margins. With ongoing trade tensions globally, the strategy has successfully offset this headwind and will continue to do so. Therefore, Dollar Tree is a BUY for long-term investors.

Q4 Results & Financial Performance

In March 2025, Dollar Tree reported earnings for Q4 2024, with mixed results. The company reported an adjusted EPS of $2.29, 4.3% higher than the $2.20 analysts projected, a YoY decline of 15.3% from the EPS in Q4 2023. However, this YoY decrease is offset by the fact that FY 2023 had a 53rd week of sales, impacting the overall figures. Meanwhile, Dollar Tree reported net sales of $5.0 billion for the quarter, far lower than the analysts’ estimates of $8.24 billion. While sales may appear significantly lower than analysts' estimates, this is due to Dollar Tree management planning to divest from the Family Dollar brand, with $3.3 billion in sales from discontinued operations from this move. From continued operations alone, however, sales in Q4 marked a YoY increase of 0.7%. According to COO Mike Creedom, the YoY net sales growth reflects “the solid comp performance and strong revenue contribution from noncomp stores.” Dollar Tree also generated an operating income of $629 million in Q4, a 15.2% YoY slide. This reflects the 2.3% reduction in gross margins and 1% rise in the adjusted SG&A rate.

The Dollar Tree management also disclosed guidance for Q1 2025 and FY 2025. The management projects an EPS of $1.10 to $1.25 for the first quarter, and $5.00 to $5.50 EPS from continued operations for FY 2025. This accounts for the $0.30-0.35 reduction in EPS for the fiscal year following the Transition Services Agreement made with Family Dollar. With the Family Dollar divestiture expected to take place in June 2025, Dollar Tree will incur the full year costs and receive reimbursement income from Family Dollar for half the year. Meanwhile, net sales between $4.5 to $4.6 billion are estimated for Q1, while the sales outlook for the year ranges from $18.5 to $19.1 billion. The net sales guidance considers the comparable store net sales growth of 3 to 5%, a healthy growth rate. With lower guidance for 2025 compared to last year, the management explains that “2025 is going to be a transitional year” following the divestiture of Family Dollar. In the wake of the tariff tensions, COO Mike Creedom clarifies that the company has several contingencies to limit the impact on the earnings and overall financials.

Moving on to the margins, the company’s gross margins have improved considerably over the past half decade, rising from 30.5% in 2020 to 35%+ in the last 3 years. Dollar Tree’s gross margins in the last 3 years are the highest since April 2015, the quarter before the Family Dollar acquisition. Meanwhile, other margins across the board have shown volatile trends in the last 5 years. The EBIT and EBIDTA margins spiked up in 2022 from the 2020 baselines of 7.4% and 10.11%, only to decrease by at least 5% and 4%, respectively. On the other hand, Dollar Tree incurred negative net margins in FY 2023 and 2024, largely caused by severe underperformance from Family Dollar chain stores. The net margin is skewed for 2024, reflecting the Family Dollar divestiture at significant short-term losses. However, with the divestiture and increase in 3.0 store locations, the management expects the margins to expand considerably in the coming quarters.

What about Dollar Tree’s efficiency and profitability over the last 5 years? Both the ROA and ROE follow a very similar trend to the company’s net margins, as described earlier: a slight increase from 2020 to 2022, followed by a plummet in 2023 and 2024. For any company, I consider an ROA of at least 5% and an ROE of at least 10% as healthy. In 2020-2022, both metrics were comfortably above their respective benchmarks. In the first half of this period, Dollar Tree maintained decent profitability and optimized its assets. However, slow earnings growth and the costs incurred to sustain the Family Dollar chain significantly affected the company’s overall net income and profitability. Hence, the Dollar Tree management acknowledged this with the divestiture and increase of 3.0 stores. This is designed to improve the company’s resource optimization and profitability in the long run.

Looking at Dollar Tree’s financial position, cash and cash equivalents came in at about $1.26 billion, against $2.43 billion in net long-term debt. This is not too much debt, considering the company has a market cap of $17.94 billion. Dollar Tree has a debt-to-equity ratio of approximately 0.86, slightly above the sector average of 0.77, indicating that Dollar Tree operates on relatively high leverage. Dollar Tree has an interest coverage ratio of approximately 14x, well above the acceptable level of 2x. Hence, Dollar Tree can comfortably pay down interest on outstanding debt. With an EBITDA of $2.04 billion and net debt of $6.57 billion in the last 12 months, Dollar Tree’s net debt-to-TTM EBITDA ratio was around 3.22. While higher than the 3.0 benchmark, the company’s debt load is not a major concern. Furthermore, Dollar Tree’s credit rating is BBB, which falls in the investment grade category. The debt shouldn’t be a major hindrance for Dollar Tree.

Long-Term Outlook

On March 25, the Dollar Tree management finally decided to divest the Family Dollar business, which will happen in June. Family Dollar will be offloaded to Brigade Capital Management and Macellum Capital Management for $1.007 billion. This is a crucial move that will relieve a significant burden from Dollar Tree’s operations. In 2015, the company acquired Family Dollar for $9 billion, with the objective that the acquisition would grow quickly compared to competitors like Dollar General (NYSE: DG) and Target (NYSE: TGT). Dollar Tree management believed the Family Tree merger would expand its consumer base and reduce costs. However, the merger became a liability on Dollar Tree’s operations, primarily since many Family Tree stores were located too close and ended up offsetting each other’s sales. Contrasting business models and strategies also played a part in reducing the customer base, creating confusion among customers about what to expect from each store, with the brand identity becoming less recognizable. As Family Dollar continued its underperformance, this became a burden on Dollar Tree’s margins with integration costs and capital being invested.

Looking forward, the Family Dollar divestiture will benefit Dollar Tree by reinvigorating a sturdy business model. Dollar Tree can now focus on growing its flagship stores over the long-term. CEO Michael Creedom is confident that the separation will enable Dollar Tree to “achieve its full potential.” Dollar Tree can shift its target customer base from lower-income clients in undeserved urban locations to middle-income customers in suburban locations. According to CEO Creedon, there is higher growth potential and demand from the higher-income group, which will play a key role in enhancing Dollar Tree’s long-term margins and stability. While Dollar Tree will have to endure short-term headwinds from the divestiture, including a $3.3 billion reduction in sales last quarter and lower EPS by $0.30 to $0.35, this will significantly reduce operating expenses and enhance profitability in the long-term horizon. Overall, Dollar Tree’s management expects the operating margin to return to pre-acquisition levels of 12%+, having reported an 11% operating margin in Q3 2024 with the divestiture yet to take place.

While Dollar Tree built a reputation for selling products at the fixed $1.25 price point, the company is implementing the Multi-Price Strategy. This year, the firm will add products at price categories of $3, $5, and $7. This strategy intends to expand its product portfolio without altering prices on existing products and expand the customer base. The chart below demonstrates that the strategy attracted more customers purchasing products in the $3 and $5 price categories. Effectively, this helped Dollar Tree gradually build a higher-income customer base. The company expanded this strategy to nearly 2900 store locations in March 2025 and plans to open 300 new stores this year. Such stores, labelled as 3.0 stores, offer customers value with their wider range of products, enhancing Dollar Tree’s profitability and margins immensely. Additional benefits of the Multi-Price Strategy include maintaining a sturdy customer appeal while mitigating the effects of inflation and cost increases caused by Trump’s tariffs.

While Trump’s tariff policies are a major short-term headwind for Dollar Tree’s revenues, with $15 to $20 million in monthly costs, CEO Creedon revealed that the Multi-Price Strategy has offset the tariff impacts by 90% in Q4. While additional tariffs on selected goods from China, Canada, and Mexico will increase monthly costs by $20 million, Dollar Tree is well-positioned to offset this. Ending FY 2024 with nearly 2900 3.0 stores, Dollar Tree aims to have 5200 stores follow the Multi-Price Strategy by the end of FY 2025. This should enable Dollar Tree to enhance its margins and continue mitigating tariff impacts by 90%+ for the remainder of the year. This year, the Dollar Tree management expects comparable-store sales growth of 3% to 5%, with this metric likely to increase further as Dollar Tree continues converting many locations into 3.0 stores.

Potential Risks

As described above, Trump’s tariffs impacted the company, increasing the prices of several products and overall costs. Approximately 43% of Dollar Tree’s direct imports come from China, a significant component of the company’s overall inventory. A higher cost of goods caused Dollar Tree’s overall costs to rise by $15 to $20 million monthly in the last quarter. Furthermore, as mentioned earlier, tariffs on products from Canada and Mexico will increase monthly costs further, impacting the company’s margins. However, the Dollar Tree management is confident that tariff impacts can be mitigated with the Multi-Price Strategy expansion. On May 12, the US and China agreed to roll back tariffs for 90 days, another mitigating factor for Dollar Tree’s cost of goods. This will lower tariffs on Dollar Tree’s direct imports from China from 145% to 30%, temporarily mitigating this headwind for Q2 and the start of Q3.

With the significant growth of e-commerce businesses in the last 10 years, Dollar Tree faces stiff competition from retail giants like Walmart and Amazon, which threaten Dollar Tree’s in-store presence and customer base. While the company launched its own e-commerce platform in 2009, Dollar Tree encountered obstacles like logistics challenges, competitor websites offering closeout merchandise or discounts, and a complicated shipping process. Competitors like Amazon offer more product variety, convenience with their “add-on” programs, and attract customers from the same target group as Dollar Tree. Additionally, while shipping products with the $1.25 price point, in several cases, Dollar Tree incurred shipping costs greater than the income generated from sales of such items. Overall, the company has been unsuccessful in expanding its presence on e-commerce platforms, potentially holding it back as competitors continue attracting customers who prefer online shopping. As digitalization and e-commerce continue to grow globally, this can potentially offset Dollar Tree’s Multi-Price strategy at in-store locations, no matter how successful the strategy becomes.

Valuation

Moving on to the DCF valuation model, I used a conservative and sustainable long-term growth rate of 3.0%, in the middle of the average growth rate range of 2% to 4%. This is used to project Dollar Tree’s future cash flows. The growth rate factors in the potential headwinds of the tariffs and e-commerce threat. I utilized a 6.0% discount rate to compute the PV of future cash flows up to 2033. As for the perpetual growth rate, I assumed a conservative rate of 2.5%. Considering all this in the DCF model, Dollar Tree’s intrinsic value is approximately $90.48. Based on the DCF estimation, Dollar Tree has an undervaluation of 5.56%.

Let’s now take a look at the Multiples Valuation Model. In the analysis, Dollar General, Target, and BJ's Wholesale Club Holdings, Inc (NYSE: BJ) are used. These three competitors operate with business models each tailored to the retail industry, with similar product portfolios and a variety of goods. DG, TGT, and BJ each have P/E ratios of 16.1, 11.29, and 26.41, respectively. Therefore, the average P/E multiple of the competitors was 17.93.

Dollar Tree’s forward EPS is 5.21, which is multiplied by the average P/E of the competitors to determine the company’s intrinsic value. With this valuation model, Dollar Tree’s estimated intrinsic value is $93.43. As of May 13, the company is slightly undervalued by 9.01%, and a P/E multiple of 16.45 below its 5-year average P/E ratio of 18.81. The DCF and Multiples Valuation Models estimate that the company is at a discount of at least 5%, with slight upside from its current share price.

Takeaway

The Dollar Tree management has taken massive strides to overturn its underperformance, with the divestiture deal of Family Dollar and the increase of 3.0 store locations. With the company still undervalued, investors have an excellent chance to invest long-term in this retailer. Dollar Tree may have to contend with rising costs of goods from tariffs and the e-commerce threat from competitors. However, Dollar Tree can mitigate the tariff headwinds and grow its operating margins to its highest levels in a decade. Dollar Tree is well-positioned to grow its flagship stores and profitability. With notable long-term growth potential, I initiate a BUY rating for Dollar Tree.

data.title: VNET: Bearish Setup But Growth Potential
data.description:
VNET Group Inc. (NASDAQ:VNET), one of China's largest internet data center providers, is having a roller coaster 2025. Shares, after languishing near all time lows for months, saw significant interest in early 2025, before crashing back. The bounce back was part of a larger rally worldwide on AI driven demand. The recent sell-off also coincides with overall corrections in tech stocks. Despite the sell-off, VNET remains above its lows of 2024. Let us check whether the fundamentals now deserve investing in VNET, for those investors willing to look beyond the risk headlines.

Earnings Analysis and Trends

Earnings numbers were encouraging for Q4 2024. Revenues grew by 18% and adjusted EBITDA also grew by 64% YoY. Guidance was also positive with revenue and adjusted EBITDA expected to grow by 10%-13% and 15-18% respectively. What was also good to note was a significant increase in capex guidance (by 100-140%) for FY 2025.

The capex increase is a bullish sign of demand increment from AI infrastructure for VNET's core IDC (Internet Data Center) segment, now seeing even higher wholesale interests driven by DeepSeeks breakaway research. In fact Q4 2024 already saw the first signs of breakaway demand from the wholesale sector, justifying an increased CapEx guidance as VNET invests in preparing infrastructure to meet those demands. Backlogs, Pipelines all look healthy.

Balance Sheet Health

The debt profile does not indicate immediate worries for VNET. Earnings are well above interest obligations. Net debt has started to show a reduction in 2024 relative to EBITDA. However, debt leverage still looks a little high. Would have been comfortable if debt to EDITDA ratio was around 4 at least. Particularly as VNET embarks on a CapEx cycle going forward. High debt load could signal refinancing issues in tightening market conditions.

Operating Cash flow is healthy, but future equity strength is strained after issuance of convertible senior notes of $600m in Q1 2024 and $430m more recently. Immediate liquidity issues seem tackled as VNET prepares for growth related investments without the need to raise debt or equity, but future equity dilutions risks, if the company does well, may spook long term investors. Generally, such risk reward tradeoffs are priced into share prices very quickly after such events. I assume future share prices reactions to this development should be less pronounced and triggered in the welcome event of the company doing well and bringing note conversion to equity into play. For now, arguing in defense of the issuance, liquidity concerns are taken off the table.

Scope for Growth

VNET’s network of data centers makes it a key player in China’s digital infrastructure push. In addition to the already ongoing emphasis on enterprise clients and wholesale IDC, future growth opportunities will hinge on serving further AI workloads, stickier contracts with hyperscalers and government bodies. VNET's CapEx cycle in 2025, investing in infrastructure development for its wholesale IDC capabilities will place VNET in a great position to grab such demand as and when traction increases. Rising demand of private deployments of DeepSeek in small to medium enterprises is expected to boost demand for VNET.

Valuation

VNET's valuation is lower than a peer like Equinix (market leader with valuation premium) and GDS (existing relations with hyperscalers). After a valuation expansion commensurate with its price jump, valuations corrected in March 2025. However, the forward EV to EBITDA ratio still looks slightly elevated compared to the last few years average. AI demand green shoots and continued growth and profitability explains a lot of that uptick. Summary, valuation shows no alarms neither opportunity. Contracts with hyperscalers could be a significant rerating trigger for multiple expansion.

Risks and Opportunities

In uncertain times, delisting risks of Chinese equity from US bourses does not look an outside risk anymore. More pertinent and immediate risks arise from China's ongoing crackdown on tech companies though, demanding more scrutiny and restrictions. Not the ideal environment for a tech company like VNET to thrive.

There are optional opportunities too. VNET has had several interests shown from private equity firms in the past. Any go private move along with restructuring or spin-offs could unlock value for existing investors, although that too comes with its own risks of specifics of the transaction.

Speculative Bet on VNET

The short interest as per Seeking Alpha, is ~10%, indicating bearishness. Equally, a support is seen at $5 from where the last bounce back initiated. Any movement below $5 could potentially trigger a short squeeze, particularly given VNET's fundamental positives beyond the risk headlines. With an upcoming earnings result, the short positions could have a say on where the shares move.

I would not recommend a buy VNET to value or long term investors because of several moderate to high concerns. The cash position and equity dilution risks, China tech crackdown and even outside risks of delisting weigh against VNET. I am rating VNET an overall "Hold" because of the bearish setup technically for long term investors till demand starts growing at even faster pace, ideally with interest from hyperscalers.

For the upcoming earnings session, keep an eye on AI fueled growth - demand acceleration will be a key positive. I am expecting a continued revenue growth in the mid teens, anything below that will not be change the hold thesis. IDC services revenues increased by 125% YoY in Q4 2024. A similar growth of ~100% YoY should be seen to gauge the health of the growing expectations from this segment.

Speculative investors can consider a "Buy" because I see growth potential, CapEx spending to prepare for incoming demand and improving topline and margins. Valuations have corrected after recent dilution risks were digested. While not a screaming buy from a valuation perspective, the valuation correction does allow for an entry at these levels. Keep a close watch on rerating triggers like hyperscaler contracts or earnings upticks on the upside and risks mentioned on the downside for exits.

data.title: FTEC: Tech ETFs Are Poised To Hit New High
data.description:
As investors fear begin to turn into a greed amid trade deals and economic certainty, the US stock market is roaring back strongly from the recent low. The uptrend is driven by beaten-down mega and large cap tech stocks due to their production and revenue generation reliance on the world's second largest economy. Furthermore, tech companies earnings growth power and potential rate cuts contribute to their upside potential. The category is also trading at attractive valuations after the recent selloff.

Despite that, choosing the right investment option is critical to generate healthy returns. Fidelity MSCI Information Technology Index ETF (NYSEARCA:FTEC) is one of the best investment vehicles with a significant concentration in tech giants. I maintain my buy rating on FTEC. Other popular tech-focused ETFs, such as iShares U.S. Technology ETF (IYW) and iShares Expanded Tech Sector ETF (IGM), appear attractive due to their exposure to key tech giants from the communication sector.

Stock Market Can Hit New Highs With Tech Stocks Leading the Uptrend

According to the CNN's Fear & Greed Index, greed is currently driving the market compared to extreme fear in the previous two months. In my recent articles during the selloff, I strongly favoured dip buying. Although it's slightly late now, I believe initiating a new position in the high beta ETFs or adding to an existing stake could be a smart strategy. This is because markets are likely to extend the uptrend amid multiple catalysts, including the economic stability, earnings growth power, potential rate cuts and attractive valuations.

The US and China agreed to reverse their recent tit for tat tariffs to de-escalate a trade war. In addition, the world's largest economies seek to conclude a long term trade deal to bring stability in global markets. While a trade deal with China is expected to fuel economic growth, it will also improve investor confidence in tech companies due to their significant exposure to China. This is also reflected in tech-heavy NASDAQ's rebound of 22% from its lowest level in early April.

Furthermore, tech companies' earnings growth power is adding to investor confidence. The tech stocks, particularly mega-caps, enabled the S&P 500 to generate 13.4% earnings growth in the first quarter, topping expectations for a 7% increase.

Of the seven mega caps, six topped March quarter estimates, with most of them were the largest contributors to the S&P 500 earnings growth. These tech giants are Alphabet (GOOG) (GOOGL), Meta (META) and NVIDIA (NVDA). Other large cap tech companies, such as Broadcom (AVGO) and Netflix (NFLX) were also the largest contributors to the S&P 500 first quarter earnings growth.

The information technology sector delivered 17% earnings growth in the first quarter while communication sector reported 29% year over year increase in earnings.

Tech stocks also appears attractive based on valuations. The recent selloff along with strong earnings growth power cooled their valuations from the recent peak. The technology sector is currently trading around 25x forward earnings compared to the recent peak of 30x. Forward PE of the Magnificent 7 declined to 25.

Moreover, the rate cuts are also expected to contribute to stock market upside and investor confidence in risky assets. In April, inflation plunged to 2.3% and unemployment rate hovered around 4.20%, in-line with the Fed's targeted range. Moreover, a slower economic growth may also persuade the Fed to cut rates. Overall, it appears that the market has bottomed-out with prospects for a strong upside in the second half of 2025.

FTEC is a Solid Option to Capitalize on the Uptrend

Fidelity MSCI Information Technology Index ETF is one of the most prominent tech-focused exchange traded funds with $12.5 billion in assets under management and 450K in average shares trading volume. FTEC's portfolio is diversified with nearly 290 stocks from the information technology sector. The fund's top 3 holdings, including Apple (AAPL), Microsoft (MSFT) and NVIDIA, make up 45% of its entire portfolio weight. FTEC's top 10 represents 60% of its portfolio concentration.

With this portfolio, the fund generated exceptional share price return for shareholders in the past five years. Its returns significantly topped the S&P 500 over the years. As Apple, NVIDIA and Microsoft plays a dominating role in FTEC's overall performance, a trade agreement with China can help its share price to rebound strongly in the coming months. Currently, Apple's shares are down more than 20% from its peak due to a trade conflict.

Apple's 90% of production is based in China while Chinese supplier make up 40% of its total supplies. Similarly, China accounts for more than 10% of NVIDIA's total revenue.

NVIDIA was among the star performers in the past two years due to its blistering revenue and earnings growth. The company was the biggest contributor to the S&P 500 earnings growth in the March quarter. Its earnings per share of $0.89 increased 82% from a year ago period. Besides the top 3 holdings, the fund also held a big stake in Broadcom, Salesforce (CRM), Palantir (PLTR), Oracle (ORCL) and Cisco (CSCO).

FTEC earned a buy rating with a high quant score of 4.22, according to the Seeking quant analysis. SA's quant system relies on the real numbers while eliminating emotions out of investment decisions. FTEC's share price momentum earned an A grade. Technically, ETFs with a high share price momentum are expected to perform well in short to long term. Its risk factor is also likely to improve given declining stock market volatility. FTEC's liquidity factor also received a strong grade.

Where Do the Peers Stand?

Other popular tech-focused ETFs are iShares U.S. Technology ETF and iShares Expanded Tech Sector ETF. IYW tracks the performance of 145 tech stocks. Like the FTEC, IYW offers a greater exposure to Apple, Microsoft and NVIDIA, making up 45% of its overall portfolio. However, IYW's portfolio also offers a small exposure to tech stocks from the communication sector, such as Meta and Alphabet. Its expense ratio of 0.39% is high while liquidity is stronger than FTEC. It also received a buy rating with a quant score of 4.21.

IGM is one of the best tech-focused ETFs due to its well-balanced portfolio. The fund offers exposure to around 290 tech stocks from the information technology and communication sector. Unlike IYW and FTEC, IGM's portfolio concentration in Apple, Microsoft and NVIDIA is limited.

In addition, it offers a higher concentration in the communication sector. I believe the greater exposure to tech stocks like Meta, Alphabet and Netflix can increase the upside potential. Meta and Alphabet and Netflix were among the biggest contributors to the S&P 500 earnings growth in the latest quarter. IGM outperformed FTEC and IYW year to date and in the last twelve months. Its quant score of 4.32 is also higher than FTEC and IYW.

In Conclusion

As fear and uncertainty is fading amid trade agreements between the largest economies, the markets are poised to recover losses and create a long-lasting uptrend. Therefore, buying the high beta tech-focused investment vehicles, such as Fidelity MSCI Information Technology Index ETF, appear to be a great strategy. The tech category's robust earnings growth power and attractive valuations adds to its upside. Other investment options like iShares U.S. Technology ETF and iShares Expanded Tech Sector ETF are also attractive due to their exposure to fast-growing communication sector.

data.title: Siemens Aktiengesellschaft 2025 Q2 - Results - Earnings Call Presentation
data.description: The following slide deck was published by Siemens Aktiengesellschaft in conjunction with their 2025 Q2 earnings call.

data.title: 2 BDCs Likely To Cut Dividends Soon
data.description:
The current macro environment for publicly traded private credit (i.e., business development companies or BDCs) has been deteriorating since the Fed started to make first base rate cuts.

Since most BDCs provide floating rate loan investments and use leverage to magnify the profit potential (i.e., spreads between cost of capital and the loan investments), lower base rates tend to be net-negative to the earnings. In other words, the benefits of cheaper cost of capital do not exceed or match the negatives of having a lower yielding asset base.

The good thing is that the current base rate levels seem to be sticky and here to stay for quite some time. The Fed has assumed a wait-and-see mode to understand how tariffs materialize in the underlying economic data. And the logic says that they (together with de-globalization efforts) should be inflationary; hence, a case for "higher for longer".

However, apart from the base rate factor, there are multiple other reasons, which have (say, from the start of this year) gradually pushed down the BDC prices across the board. For example, currently, the sector median P/NAV multiple is 0.85x, which means that we can buy many BDCs at a 15% discount to their net asset values. The key reasons or drivers here are:

  • Private credit is becoming more popular among institutional investors that stimulates a massive new capital formation in the sector.
  • The new capital coming in has not been matched with the rising demand. Instead, the demand has weakened due to shallow M&A markets (driven by elevated uncertainty). This has led to spread-tightening in the sector.
  • The risks in the economy have increased, triggering a "risk-off" movement. Namely, BDCs as a high-risk asset class has gone increasingly out of vogue as investors try to de-risk their portfolio holdings amid the elevated uncertainty in the system. The underlying risk or assumption here is that in this kind of environment, BDCs should eventually be recognizing growing non-accruals that should cause significant NAV declines and impaired dividends.

The overall macro backdrop is not positive for BDCs.

And let me insert a quote from Warren Buffett here:

Only when the tide goes out do you discover who's been swimming naked

So, what this kind of environment does is crystallizes investment vehicles that are walking on a thin ice and have not really been properly prepared to sustain value in case of more material headwinds.

For many BDCs the most immediate practical implications will translate to dividend reductions as they will have to synchronize the distributions with declining cash generation levels (e.g., non-accruals reducing cash-flowing loan investment base, spread compression etc.)

In this context, I would like to elaborate on two practical BDC examples that, in my view, are about to cut their dividends already this year.

#1 - Horizon Technology Finance, 17.5%

Horizon Technology Finance (NASDAQ:HRZN) is a venture capital focused BDC that has experienced significant price declined over the past couple of quarters. As dividends have remained unchanged but the price has declined, the yield has jumped higher accordingly. Currently, the offered yield stands at 17.5%, which is one of the highest in the sector. And I would say that it is too good to be true.

Here is why.

The structural backdrop is not favorable for BDCs, just as I mentioned above in the intro section. However, for venture capital BDCs, the current environment brings more severe consequences on the underlying operations. In this case, when we have a risk-off mode in the system and the M&A activity is subdued, it is very difficult to source in healthy deal volumes at the necessary scale and quality. Plus, since VCs usually operate with thinner margin of safety than regular and already established businesses, they are more exposed to corporate default risks.

But, obviously, there are several additional HRZN-specific aspects that have pushed down the share price.

First, in Q1, 2025, HRZN generated a NII per share of $0.27, which is in line with the result achieved in the prior quarter despite the fact that the leverage load has increased. What matters here is that the NII per share does not cover the base dividend of $0.33 per share. It means that HRZN has to use leverage to close the dividend coverage gap. This could work in the short term, but it is clearly unsustainable in the long-run. To solve the issue here, HRZN has to either grow the NII or decrease the dividend. In my view, it will have to be the latter move.

Second, HRZN has a significant difference between the portfolio debt yield and what it can source from the markets. Namely, the new loan onboarding yields come in at just below 13%, while the current portfolio debt yield stands at 15%. Over time, the portfolio yield should converge to the market-level, which implies further headwinds to the NII generation.

Third, HRZN has really no options to utilize additional debt to fund new investments that would produce accretive spreads to the NII generation. Currently, HRZN has a debt to equity of 1.54x, which is the sixth highest (most aggressive) ratio in the sector.

In a nutshell, I do not see how HRZN could solve the dividend coverage shortfall situation without reducing the dividend itself.

#2 - Monroe Capital, 16.3%

Monroe Capital (NASDAQ:MRCC) is another high-yielding BDC that has been experiencing considerable price declines over the past couple of quarters. And even though the share price has plunged by more than 25% on a YTD basis, the dividend has remained unchanged, which has resulted in a quite attractive current yield of 16.3%.

Now, the reason why MRCC has suffered so much is mainly attributable to too aggressive leverage in combination with a weak portfolio quality (i.e., notable non-accruals and unrealized losses).

In my view, the situation here is worse than in HRZN's case because at least HRZN has the embedded potential to monetize some part of its warrant/equity base that could generate tangible non-core cash income.

Let me explain.

MRCC distributes a quarterly base dividend of $0.25 per share. However, the NII per share based on Q1, 2025 result is only $0.19. In the prior quarter, the NII per share was $0.23 (adjusted for some positive one-offs). The trajectory is bad, just as the current situation on the dividend coverage end. The dividend coverage is 0.76x, which is the absolute worst level in the entire BDC space.

The question is whether MRCC can recover from it. In my opinion, it is doable, but only if some serious dividend cuts are made.

Currently, the debt to equity is 1.45x, which gives no room for additional growth. In 2026, MRCC will have to refinance $130 million of fixed rate note that has a yield of 4.7%. It means that MRCC will have to recognize a higher cost of capital in 2026.

The matters are made worse by the presence of a notable payment-in-kind income stream that is effectively a non-cash like item, making the true cash dividend coverage less than 0.76x.

Given these fundamental nuances, I do not really see how MRCC could suddenly start generating higher NII per share to hit a sustainable level of dividend coverage.

The bottom line

Even though I am a BDC-bull, I think that the prevailing macro environment does not bode well for the sector in general.

In practice, it means that the total returns will be lower than usual (say, from 2021 to 2022) across the board. More importantly, this should also trigger many dividend reductions in the sector as the BDCs themselves try to align the lower asset yields with sustainable dividend levels.

HRZN and MRCC are two great examples of where, in my opinion, we will see some dividend cuts very soon.

data.title: From Oversold To Overbought In Record Time
data.description:
The stock market has swung from an extremely oversold condition six weeks ago to one that is arguably overbought. By the same token, outlooks from market pundits and Wall Street strategists have been equally emotional, swinging from warnings of recession and bear market to targets of new all-time highs for the S&P 500. That is the kind of sensationalism that attracts attention and furthers the extremes, but reality is more often something in between. After a 22% surge in the S&P 500 index since April’s intra-day low, the stock market looks tired, and I think we are overdue for a pullback of as much as 10% to 5,300 this summer. That doesn’t necessitate a recession or a bear market, but a reset as we move into fall.

Yesterday, I discussed the negative rates of change I expect to see in the high frequency economic data, including inflation, consumer spending, economic growth, and employment. These changes will be instigated by the Trump administration’s trade policy. The headwinds will eventually be countered by the economic tailwinds of deregulation and continued easing in monetary policy by the Fed during the second half of the year. But the headwinds will strike first, which is why I think the next couple of months could be sobering for investors who are chasing this rally.

I think valuations are a horrible timing tool, because stocks can stay expensive and become more expensive, irrespective of valuations, so long as the rates of change are moving in a favorable direction. When they start to deteriorate, the potential downside has everything to do with valuations, and we are clearly stretched at current levels. With the S&P 500 closing at 5,892 the index is now trading at 22x forward earnings of $265. If estimates were increasing and rate of change were improving, that might be reasonable, but they are not.

Additionally, the $265 is based on an acceleration in the rate of earnings and revenue growth over the coming three quarters. Considering the headwinds that tariffs present to the rate of economic growth between now and year end, that seems impossible, which means we are likely to see downward revisions. That is another negative rate of change, and they seem to be stacking up.

We never know what will instigate the selling from an overbought condition, but my bet is that it will be the bond market, which seems to be growing increasingly concerned about how serious the Trump administration is about reducing our runaway deficit. That was a major campaign promise that DOGE was supposed to tackle with a proclaimed $2 trillion in spending cuts from Elon Musk. The reality is that the cuts have amounted to less than $100 million in savings.

Now we are moving on to tax cuts, which will result in a loss of revenue that dwarves the savings realized by DOGE and increases the debt, doing little to put a dent into deficits that have been in excess of 6% over the past two years. This raises concerns with bond investors, as the 10-year Treasury yield climbs above 4.5% and the 30-year yield approaches 5%. If these yields continue to climb above key levels in repudiation of broken promises to reign in government spending, it could be the trigger that leads to a wave of profit taking in the stock market, resolving our overbought condition.

data.title: AMD: Something Bigger Than The $6B Buyback Comes Next Month
data.description:
Investment Thesis

This week offered one of the first real glimpses of light in almost two months of uncertainty created by the back-and-forth narrative surrounding global trade and chip export restrictions.

The news of a trade war hiatus between the US/China has offered markets some relief on how to navigate the tricky environment for many companies like Advanced Micro Devices, Inc. (NASDAQ:AMD), whose revenue and operations are exposed to China. The positivity from the news of the trade war hiatus follows another major development from last week, where the Trump 2.0 administration vowed to end the AI Diffusion interim rule and “alter export curb controls,” which should be another boost to chip companies such as AMD. There is also the recent $10B partnership struck this week, which boosted the stock. Further is AMD’s announcement of a massive $6B buyback plan which I believe is important for shareholders.

In my view, the most important updates critical in fundamentally changing AMD’s outlook will come next month at the Advancing AI event, where the company is widely expected to introduce the next iteration of its AI accelerator, the MI350 GPU. In addition, AMD should also be announcing big updates to its software stack and the roadmap to its rack-scale solutions.

I believe AMD is well positioned to advance its AI accelerator model and gain share. The company is already making strides, as seen in its Q1 report, and I am maintaining my Strong Buy rating on the company, noting the unjustified pessimism surrounding the stock.

AMD’s Business Inflection Sees Strong Upward Currents

I have been extremely bullish in all my previous coverages on AMD, but so far markets have not rewarded AMD’s investors (myself included) as I had expected. In my last post on AMD, I detailed how I believed 2025 would be a transformative year for the Santa Clara, CA-based chip maker, and the Q1 report is almost exactly what I was looking for.

Every other segment that was not a Data Center business has continued to report strong signs of inflection in their businesses, as seen in the y/y growth rates for AMD’s non-Data Center business segments below.

Embedded and Gaming segments continue to report strong inflection. On the Q1 earnings call, AMD’s management strongly hinted that the Embedded business should finally deliver y/y gains in H2, with management observing improvement in book-to-bill ratios while many end markets, such as aerospace and defense, were waking up with stronger demand signals. AMD’s success story continues in the Client business with the PC market showing strong signs of growth towards AMD’s favor.

Then there is also the widely followed market research report from Jon Peddie that shows AMD gained share versus Nvidia (NASDAQ:NVDA) in terms of Q4 discrete GPU shipment volumes. The Q4 share gain of 17% in the discrete GPU market was one of the largest ever by AMD, a total of 700 bp from the preceding quarter.

At the center of AMD’s growth story is its Data Center business which reported strong revenues of $3.67B, 57% higher than the previous year and just edging out the $3.66B that markets were expecting. On an annualized basis, AMD’s Data Center business is now a ~$15B business crucial to its revenue base. AMD’s MI300 AI accelerator was crucial to the company delivering such strong growth since launching in December 2023.

Looking ahead, MI350 will now assume the lead breadwinner in AMD’s $15B data center business.

What’s Next For AMD? ROCm & Rack-Scale At Advancing AI?

Management has actually pulled in the release schedule of its MI350 accelerator chip and will announce it to the world at the Advancing AI event next month. The reasons for pulling in the release of its MI350 accelerator were already revealed in an earlier earnings call where management saw strong demand during pilots and sampling phases with certain key customers.

AMD has been working on changes to its ROCm software stack to better compete with CUDA and is working towards advancing the community around ROCm. In a sit-down with a popular semiconductor newsletter, AMD’s management, especially the technical leadership, is working towards better developing the community around ROCm. According to the newsletter, “AMD plans to take a page out of Google’s TRC playbook (TPU Research Cloud) and launch a developer cloud at their upcoming Advancing AI event in June."

I also noted how AMD added the MI300 into PyTorch’s quicker software development capabilities that should give better time-to-market benefits for AMD’s customers who use the MI300x. I’m quite confident MI350x will be added as well upon launch. This was also confirmed by AMD per the newsletter. The newsletter also hints at AMD announcing more updates about how the ZT Systems integration will play a bigger role in AMD’s rack-scale solutions, which could be seen with the MI400x and above models to be launched next year.

AMD has also won a huge contract for delivering 30,000 MI355x accelerators, which could be for OpenAI’s Stargate via Oracle (NYSE:ORCL). This was later confirmed by AMD’s CEO, Lisa Su, on the Q1 earnings call. This could be one of the largest orders for AMD’s AI accelerators, which should boost the visibility of its accelerator chips.

AMD is also making strong commitments to investing more dollars into its software development teams and hiring the right personnel. The ZT Systems "acqui-hire" deal was designed exactly along these lines—to bolster their expertise in rack-scale-level solutions. And the elevated investment levels also confirm AMD’s plans to build growth runways to better compete with Nvidia.

All these developments make me believe AMD will do much better than markets expect. The current environment around AMD still continues to be unjustifiably pessimistic in my opinion.

AMD’s management also understands that and this could be one of the reasons why the company yesterday announced a $6B stock buyback program which when compared to its current market capitalization of ~$182B implies the company can buyback ~3% of the company’s shares outstanding as of today. But the positive news that I expect from AMD’s event next month means that the updates from the Advancing AI event could be a meaningful tailwind to the company for years.

AMD has already started to perform quite well and the Q1 report manifested some of that performance we should start to see in AMD. In the chart below, investors can see the magnitude of the earnings beat that AMD delivered in Q1. The Q1 earnings report delivered by AMD was one of the largest earnings beats in over 8 quarters.

The only caveat in all of this is how markets price in AMD’s $1.5B headwind that I discuss below.

AMD’s China Exposure & Its $1.5B Headwind

AMD has put investors on notice that it will be taking an $800M hit due to the inventory of MI308 chips, which were made exclusively for the China market. This $800M hit, which it will absorb next quarter, implies that its gross margins will take a 10 percent sequential hit, which will be a big pill for investors to swallow.

It doesn’t end there.

AMD further advised investors that for the full year, it could possibly take a $1.5B hit due to inventory related to products made specifically for the China market, which is sure to further impact its margins.

But the recent developments surrounding the removal of chip export restrictions cited in the AI Diffusion rule are a strong positive legislative step in the right direction for chip companies such as AMD.

AMD’s dominant archrival, Nvidia, already announced its own $5.5B inventory-related charges to its margin profile last month but has reportedly already started working on new China-specific chips. In March, AMD’s MI309, the next version of MI308, was reportedly rejected. It is only a matter of time before AMD also starts to work on their own revised versions of the MI300X China-specific chip.

With the May 15 compliance date for the AI Diffusion rule definitely scrapped, I expect the Trump 2.0 administration to publish clearer industry-first rules that articulate export rules without dampening industry sales, which should help chip companies like AMD. This should most likely alleviate the $1.5B headwind that AMD is seeing.

AMD Is Quite Undervalued With Elevated Pessimism

I believe markets have still not revised AMD’s outlook fully given its strong Q1 report and the positive developments from the US/China trade negotiations from the last 3-4 days.

AMD’s revenue is expected to rise ~23% in CY25 to ~$31.6B. These estimates have fallen by ~3% over the last six months, but analysts have been positively revising their outlook on AMD over the past month by 1.4%.

AMD’s CY25 EPS, on the other hand, has been revised lower by 15% over the past three months, with markets expecting AMD’s earnings to grow 21% to $4 a share. Naturally, this is because AMD guided investors towards its worst-case scenario of taking a $1.5B hit to margins.

Given that I expect AMD to navigate past the $1.5B headwind successfully, I estimate AMD could see a ~60-70 cent boost to its earnings per share. Markets have not priced that in at the moment, in my opinion. The 60-70 cents boost implies an EPS growth rate closer to the mid-30s.

In such a situation, AMD’s earnings growth would actually be in the mid-thirties, implying that its current valuation of 28x earnings is a steal.

Even without the assumptions of the 60-70 boost that I am expecting, AMD’s stock trades at attractive valuations as compared to its recent history.

Risks & Other Factors To AMD’s Outlook

I used the word “hiatus” for the developments in US/China trade negotiations because both sides have agreed to hold off slapping tariffs on one another for 90 days. The ideal scenario is that these talks yield inconclusive results that suit both countries, which will be a big boost for AMD’s outlook. The worst-case scenario in these talks is that trade negotiations fail. In that case, higher tariffs are back on the table. The other risk is if AMD is forced to write off inventory charges more than the $1.5B it has already announced. That could happen in a situation if export controls become even more stringent under the current US administration.

Regardless, I have been increasing my exposure to AMD over the past six months, and I’m confident AMD should be navigating those risks effortlessly.

Takeaway

I believe markets have priced in the worst for AMD after the company announced the $1.5B hit due to its China-related inventory. In my opinion, markets will transition to looking ahead towards the Advancing AI event in early June, which should see the MI350 chip being launched as well as some strong updates to AMD’s software stack.

Moreover, AMD's other business segments have begun to fire up their inflection cylinders which is even more reassuring in the overall growth outlook for AMD.

I continue to recommend a Strong Buy on AMD and keep adding to my position.

data.title: Celsius Holdings Bottom Is Here - Growth Setup Looks Promising
data.description:
CELH's Reversal May Already Be Here Thanks To The Alani Acquisition, As The Bulls Also Defend A Floor

We previously covered Celsius Holdings, Inc. (NASDAQ:CELH) in April 2025, discussing how the stock's trading floor had seemingly materialized, thanks to the robust performance metrics reported in FY2024 and the accretive Alani Nu acquisition.

Combined with the rich Free Cash Flow generation, the promising consensus forward estimates, and the reasonable valuations, the stock may deliver more than doubling upside potential ahead.

CELH 1Y Stock Price

Since then, CELH has failed to break out of its next resistance levels of $37s ranges as expected, albeit seemingly well-supported at its 50/ 200 day moving averages.

Much of its tailwinds are naturally attributed to the recent closing of the Alani Nu acquisition on April 01, 2025 and the bottoming of its performance metrics after the painful PepsiCo (PEP) inventory adjustments since May 2024.

For reference, CELH reported overall FQ1'25 revenues of $329.3M (-0.8% QoQ/ -7.4% YoY), adj EBITDA margins of 21.1% (+2.2 points QoQ/ -3.6 YoY), and adj EPS of $0.18 (+28.5% QoQ/ -33.3% YoY).

These numbers mark its second consecutive quarter of improved top/ bottom-line performances after the trough FQ3'24 numbers of $265.7M (-33.9% QoQ/ -30.9% YoY), 1.6% (-23.3 points QoQ/ -25.3 YoY), and $0.00 (compared to a quarter ago at $0.28 / a year ago at $0.30), respectively.

While CELH continues to report a sustained impact of PEP's inventory correction in the North American region's revenues of $306.5M in FQ1'25 (-1.7% QoQ/ -9.7% YoY), it is apparent that the headwind may be occurring across the board, as the sugar-free energy drink competition intensifies and the promotional activities increase.

The same has been observed in Monster Beverage's (MNST) decelerating sales growth in the UCAN region at $4.32B in FY2024 (+2.8% YoY), compared to FY2023 levels at $4.2B (+10.5% YoY).

Otherwise, CELH continues to report higher US energy drink category dollar share of 16.2% on a pro forma basis in Q1'25 (+1.9 points YoY), based on Celsius at 10.9% (-0.5 points YoY) and Alani Nu at 5.3% (+2.4 points YoY), compared to MNST at 27.6% (-0.7 points YoY) and Red Bull at 37.1% (+0.4 points YoY).

These reasons are also why we believe that the Alani Nu acquisition is likely to be highly strategic while accretive to its overall energy drink portfolio's performance/ financial metrics from Q2'25 onwards.

At the same time, CELH has been able to successfully scale its international presence as well, as observed in the growing international revenues of $22.8M (+12.3% QoQ/ +40.7% YoY) across the legacy EMEA markets and the newly launched markets, including the UK, Ireland, France, Australia, and New Zealand.

Combined with the improving bottom-lines, new flavors, ongoing promotional events for multi-pack offerings, growing retail shelf space by between +15% to +20%, and new secondary placements near checkout lines, we can understand why the market is cautiously optimistic about its future recovery, as observed in the stock holding on to much of its recent March/ April 2025 gains.

The Consensus Forward Estimates

For now, it is unsurprising why the consensus has already raised their forward estimates, with CELH expected to chart an accelerated top/ bottom-line growth at a CAGR of +28.6%/ +26.4% through FY2027, compared to the prior estimates of +14.7%/ +22.2%, respectively, likely attributed to the accretive Alani Nu acquisition from FQ2'25 onwards.

On the other hand, it is undeniable that CELH's growth profile is already maturing compared to its 5Y historical growth at +79.5%/ +103.6%, respectively.

This is worsened by the potential tariff impact on the aluminum cans and its COGS/ gross margins from H2'25 onwards, despite the regionalized manufacturing capabilities and the prior acquisition of Big Beverages' manufacturing facility in the US.

Given that CELH's inventory correction is likely to continue through Q2'25 before easing in H2'25, the company may potentially underperform in FY2025 as well, against its pro forma FY2024 revenues of $1.95B and adj EBITDA of $392.7M along with our prior FY2027 adj EBITDA estimates of $880M (3Y CAGR of +31%).

On the other hand, the tariff war has shown promising signs of de-escalation with the UK and China, amongst others, with it signaling that the worst of the recessionary/ stagflation fears may already be behind us.

CELH Valuations

For now, the decelerating growth trends may also be why the CELH stock has been discounted to FWD EV/ EBITDA valuations of 21.85x and our estimated FWD P/E non-GAAP valuations of 41.46x, based on the $37.32 stock prices at the time of writing and the consensus FY2025 adj EPS estimates of $0.90 (+28.5% YoY).

This is compared to its 1Y mean of 26.13x/ 40.55x and the 5Y mean of 106.62x/ 316.60x, albeit up from the recent bottom of 16.92x/ 25.33x, respectively.

On the one hand, our estimated CELH's FWD P/E non-GAAP valuations of 41.46x may seem to be somewhat expensive, when compared to the sector median of 16.18x and the energy drinks/ beverage peers, including PEP at 16.37x, Coca-Cola Company (KO) at 23.78x, and Keurig Dr Pepper (KDP) at 16.48x, aside from MNST at 32.80x.

On the other hand, based on our estimated FWD P/E non-GAAP valuations of 41.46x and the consensus adj EPS growth at a CAGR of +26.4%, CELH appears to be cheap at FWD PEG non-GAAP ratio of 1.57x, compared to its 5Y mean of 3.05x.

When compared to the sector median FWD PEG non-GAAP ratio of 2.63x and the energy drinks/ beverage peers, including MNST at 2.69x, PEP at 4.78x, KO at 3.93x, and KDP at 2.29x, it is apparent that CELH remains a compelling high-growth investment thesis here.

So, Is CELH Stock A Buy, Sell, or Hold?

CELH 5Y Stock Price

For now, CELH has already lost -60.4% or the equivalent -$12.78B of its market capitalization from the 2024 heights, albeit seemingly finding a floor at the February 2025 bottom of $21 ranges.

For context, we had previously offered a bull-case long-term price target of $79.30 in our prior article, based on the projected expansion in its adj EBITDA per share to $3.70 by FY2027 (expanding at a CAGR of +30.8%) and the prior FWD EV/ EBITDA valuations of 21.44x.

With it remaining to be seen when CELH's inventory correction may last, worsened by the intermediate term uncertainty surrounding the intensified tariff war, it appears that our prior estimates may have been overly aggressive, as the consensus also projects a much slower 3Y adj EBITDA growth at a pro forma CAGR of +17.8% ahead.

Despite so, based on our estimated FWD P/E non-GAAP valuations of 41.46x and the consensus' current FY2027 adj EPS estimates of $1.41 (expanding at a 3Y CAGR of +26.4%), we are still looking at a relatively promising upside potential of +56.4% to our long-term price target of $58.40.

These estimates do not seem to be overly aggressive as well, given Alani Nu's FY2024 top-line growth by +62.7% YoY, with it likely to jump start CELH's stagnant growth profile.

Combined with the materialization of bullish support at the prior $21 ranges and the moderating short interest volumes by -30.3% from recent heights, we are cautiously reiterating our Buy rating for the CELH stock.

data.description:

BBG U.S. Govt./Mortgage2 2.96 2.96 4.84 0.21 -1.24 1.04

Expenses for Institutional shares: Total 1.52%; Net, Including Investment Related Expenses (dividend expense, interest expense, acquired fund fees and expenses and certain other fund expenses) 1.20%. For Investor A shares: Total 1.77%; Net, Including Investment Related Expenses 1.45%. Institutional and Investor A shares have contractual waivers with an end date of 06/30/2026 terminable upon 90 days' notice. For certain share classes, BlackRock may voluntarily agree to waive certain fees and expenses in which the adviser may discontinue at any time without notice. Expenses stated as of the fund's most recent prospectus. Data represents past performance and is no guarantee of future results. Investment returns and principal values may fluctuate so that an investor's shares, when redeemed, may be worth more or less than their original cost. All returns assume reinvestment of dividends and capital gains. Current performance may be lower or higher than that shown. Refer to Investment Management & Financial Services | BlackRock for most recent month-end performance. Investment returns reflect total fund operating expenses, net of all fees, waivers and/or expense reimbursements. Index performance is shown for illustrative purposes only. It is not possible to invest directly in an unmanaged index. Share classes have different sales charges, fees and other features. Returns with sales charge reflect deduction of current maximum initial sales charge of 4% for Investor A shares. Institutional shares have no front- or back-end load. Institutional shares have limited availability and may be purchased at various minimums. See prospectus for details. Net Expenses Excluding Investment Related Expenses for Institutional shares: 0.45%; for Investor A shares: 0.70%.

Commentary as of 03/31/25

  • The fund posted returns of 3.02% (Institutional shares) and 2.95% (Investor A shares, without sales charge) for the first quarter of 2025.
  • Allocations to agency collateralized mortgage obligations (CMOs) drove the fund's positive performance, alongside positioning in single-asset/single-borrower (SASB) commercial mortgage-backed securities (CMBS). Long-basis positions in agency mortgage-backed securities with high coupons (6.0%) and low coupons (2.5% and 3.0%) weighed on returns.
  • The fund maintained a modest position in Ginnie Mae 30-year MBS versus conventional MBS with a coupon of 5.5%, as well as allocations to floating rate agency CMOs and agency mortgage derivatives. At quarter-end, headline duration (interest rate sensitivity) was flat to slightly long relative to the benchmark, while the fund had an underweight exposure to conventional 30-year MBS, an overweight allocation to Ginnie Mae 30-year MBS, and a modestly underweight position in conventional 15-year MBS.

Contributors

Out-of-benchmark exposures to agency mortgage derivatives, specifically interest-only allocations, were the primary driver of performance. Allocations to SASB CMBS performed positively, as did tactical short agency MBS basis expressions with a 4.5% coupon.

Detractors

Long-basis expressions in 30-year conventional MBS with coupons of 2.5%, 3.5%, and 6.0% detracted modestly. Within impact MBS pools, lower-coupon rural housing, low area median income, and Housing Finance Agency (HFA) pools underperformed non-impact MBS pools, such as loan balance pools.

Portfolio management

Matthew Kraeger, Siddharth Mehta

Top 10 holdings (%)

GNMA II 55.09
Federal National Mortgage 28.30
FHLM 26.62
BLK Liquidity Funds 1.35
Gnma_20-149h-la 0.91
Fhlmc_5444c-Fc 0.74
Fnma_23-46a-Fa 0.47
Gnma_21-159-Ih 0.43
Federal Home Loan Mortgage Corporation -Gold 0.42
Gnma_22-127c-la 0.41

Investment approach

Invests primarily in the highest rated government and agency bonds and maintains an average portfolio duration within ±20% of the Bloomberg Intermediate Government Index.

Further insight

At quarter-end, the fund maintained a concentration in agency MBS pools, with a core allocation to impact MBS collateral, such as manufactured housing, down-payment assistance, and state HFA pools. This was done because their collateral attributes exhibit a meaningful benefit in prepayment characteristics through factors like loan size and seasoning. Within agency MBS relative value expressions, we have shifted from an up-in-coupon bias toward a more "barbell" approach, in which we favor conventional high coupons of 6.0% and low coupons of 2.5% and 3.0% versus middle coupons such as 4.5%.

Important Risks: The fund is actively managed and its characteristics will vary. Holdings shown should not be deemed as a recommendation to buy or sell securities. Bond values fluctuate in price so the value of your investment can go down depending on market conditions. Fixed income risks include interest-rate and credit risk. Typically, when interest rates rise, there is a corresponding decline in bond values. Credit risk refers to the possibility that the bond issuer will not be able to make principal and interest payments. Principal of mortgage- or asset-backed securities normally may be prepaid at any time, reducing the yield and market value of those securities. Obligations of U.S. gov't agencies are supported by varying degrees of credit but generally are not backed by the full faith and credit of the U.S. gov't. The fund may use derivatives to hedge its investments or to seek to enhance returns. Derivatives entail risks relating to liquidity, leverage and credit that may reduce returns and increase volatility.

The opinions expressed are those of the fund's portfolio management team as of March 31, 2025, and may change as subsequent conditions vary. Information and opinions are derived from proprietary and nonproprietary sources deemed by BlackRock to be reliable, are not necessarily all-inclusive and are not guaranteed as to accuracy.

BlackRock provides compensation in connection with obtaining or using third-party ratings and rankings.

1 Class R shares are sold to a limited group of investors, including certain retirement plans. See prospectus for details. 2 The Bloomberg U.S. Government/Mortgage Index measures the performance of U.S. government bonds and mortgage-related securities; the Bloomberg U.S. Agency Index, which includes native currency agency debentures from issuers such as Fannie Mae (FNMA), Freddie Mac (FHLMC) and Federal Home Loan Bank; and the Bloomberg U.S. MBS Index, which includes U.S. agency mortgage-backed pass-through securities (both fixed-rate and hybrid ARM) issued by Ginnie Mae (GNMA), FNMA and FHLMC.

You should consider the investment objectives, risks, charges and expenses of the fund carefully before investing. data.title: Realty Income: Shareholders Deserve To Be Rewarded With More Upside
data.description:
With a market capitalization of $50.95 billion, Realty Income (NYSE:O) is a giant in the REIT space. Over the years, the company has achieved remarkable growth, with acquisitions of other major players in the industry helping to make that possible. Recently, the quality of the business has been on display. You see, back in February of this year, I decided to revisit the firm. This came after shares had dipped slightly despite will sting continued growth in revenue. At the time, I argued that the small drop the stock experienced, combined with the quality of the business and how shares were priced, justified a bullish outlook. Ultimately, I ended up reaffirming my ‘buy’ rating on it.

Since the time that article was published, Realty Income has outperformed the broader market. Shares are up 3.2% at a time when the S&P 500 is down 5.4%. This comes on the back of growth in revenue, profits, and cash flows. The current trajectory that the company is on looks promising and, with net leverage in the same range as other players, it is certainly deserving of attention. Admittedly, shares are not as cheap as some of the other players in the industry and those firms might make for better prospects. But just because one company might be better does not mean that another doesn't justify a bullish outlook. Because of these factors, I believe that maintaining the company as a soft ‘buy’ makes sense at this time.

Taking another look at Realty Income

Since my last article about Realty Income, investors have been provided with data covering one additional fiscal quarter. That happens to be the first quarter of the company's 2025 fiscal year. During that time, the company saw revenue come in at $1.38 billion. That's an increase of 9.5% compared to the $1.26 billion reported the previous year. This was driven largely by a $90.2 million rise in rental revenue that the company generated.

On a same store basis, revenue grew from $1.14 billion to $1.15 billion. This increase of $14.3 million came from lease renewals and rent escalations that the business received from tenants. An even bigger driver of these sales increased during this time came from new properties acquired between 2024 and 2025. All told, the company purchased 497 properties that added a combined $52.4 million of additional revenue on a year-over-year basis. Of course, in order to fund these purchases, management also divested of certain assets.

This included 361 properties that were sold during the relevant window of time. This negatively impacted revenue to the tune of $12.4 million. After factoring in these sales, Realty Income saw the number of properties it has grown from 15,485 to 15,627. It is worth noting that, year over year, the enterprise saw its occupancy rate remain almost flat, dipping ever so slightly from 98.6% to 98.5%. In the chart below, you can see how consistent occupancy has been over the last couple of years. This serves as a testament to the high-quality nature of the assets that the company has and its track record of selling off assets that no longer make sense for its portfolio.

The growth in revenue also brought with it a rise in profitability. I maintain that net income is not all that relevant when it comes to REITs. But for illustrative purposes, from the first quarter of 2024 to the first quarter of 2025, net profits for the company improved from $129.7 million to $249.8 million. Obviously, the rise in revenue contributed here. But another contributor was the fact that, in the first quarter of 2024, the company incurred $94.1 million in merger, transaction, and other related costs, that were related to its acquisition of Spirit Realty Capital in a deal valued at $6.19 billion. This excluded the net debt that Spirit Realty Capital had on its books.

Other profitability metrics rose as well. Operating cash flow ticked up modestly from $778.7 million to $787.5 million. If we adjust for changes in working capital, we would get a rise from $785.4 million to $941.4 million. There are other profitability metrics that we should take into consideration as well. As an example, there is FFO, or funds from operations. This metric expanded from $785.7 million to $937.7 million. On an adjusted basis, it grew from $862.9 million to $949.7 million. And finally, EBITDA for the business went from $1.15 billion to $1.26 billion.

This is a fantastic showing for the company and serves as a testament to its quality and growth prospects. For the rest of this year, management has provided some guidance, but not much. On a per share basis, adjusted FFO should be around $4.22 to $4.28. At the midpoint, this would imply reading of $3.80 billion. For context, last year, this figure came in at $3.62 billion. If we assume that other profitability metrics will rise at the same rate, we would expect FFO to be around $3.64 billion, adjusted operating cash flow to be $3.72 billion, and EBITDA to total $5.06 billion.

The year-over-year growth can certainly be attributed in part to the aforementioned acquisition of Spirit Realty Capital. However, management continues to invest in growing its asset base. In fact, this year, the company is planning to allocate around $4 billion toward acquisition activities. For the first quarter of this year, the company has already made purchases of $1.18 billion. This excludes $22.9 million in improvements allocated toward real estate that it already owns and another $5.3 million that it has invested in unconsolidated entities. Investments in loans also came in at $200.9 million. This doesn't mean that the company is only interested in buying. As I mentioned earlier, it's not afraid to sell assets. But during the quarter, it only divested of $92.6 million worth of properties.

If we use the estimates that we have calculated for 2025, and we also use historical results for 2024, we can see in the chart above how shares of Realty Income are currently priced. Typically, these are multiples that I would expect a ‘hold’ candidate to have, not a ‘buy’. However, I do allow some wiggle room for high-quality companies, especially if they are continuing to grow. Relative to similar companies, Realty Income is perhaps slightly on the high side of the spectrum. In the table below, I compared it to five such firms. On a price to operating cash flow basis, I found that four of the five companies ended up being cheaper than Realty Income is. And on an EV to EBITDA basis, this number totaled three of the five.

Company Price / Operating Cash Flow EV / EBITDA
Realty Income 14.4 16.2
Simon Property Group (SPG) 14.0 15.3
Kimco Realty (KIM) 13.3 16.5
Regency Centers (REG) 16.8 18.2
Federal Realty Investment Trust (FRT) 12.9 15.5
NNN REIT (NNN) 11.9 14.7

I would like to point out, however, that the range of multiples for these companies really is not that vast. This is especially true when it comes to the EV to EBITDA approach. As an example, if Realty Income were to trade at the lowest multiple of the five competitors, then the EV to EBITDA approach would imply downside of 13.8%. The downside using the price to operating cash flow approach would be a bit more at 17.3%. By comparison, using the highest multiple of the five companies, upside would be 16.7% for the price to operating cash flow scenario, and it would be 19.3% when it comes to the EV to EBITDA approach. This places the company pretty solidly in the middle of the range compared to these other firms.

Another thing to keep in mind is that the net leverage ratio of the business is within range of the competition as well. In the chart below, you can see that its net leverage ratio is 5.54. Only two of the five competitors are lower than our candidate is at this time. But the overall range in difference between these six firms is remarkably small, indicating that at least they are all of a similar level of safety.

Takeaway

At the end of the day, I believe that Reality Income is a solid business that will continue to grow as time goes on. It might not be the most attractive opportunity, even in its own space. But between the quality that the company has exhibited and its continued growth, I believe that its current pricing justifies a soft ‘buy’ rating right now.
